item.content:encoded:
Thank you for reading, everyone. Thank you for your eyeballs, the moments of your time that you cannot recover in your mortal lives, the access of my mind into your mind. Thank you for enjoying that which I expel out to the world.

You have my gratitude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No idea whodunnit but this is really cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea who killed him, but damn am I enjoying trying to puzzle it out. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
okay, so I freaking love this. good, simple, decent length — not too short or long, and solid work. a pleasure to read.

spoilery questions w/quotes from the article:

+ show block
– hide block

" I spotted a firearm3 floating between himself and the ceiling, with a bullet frozen in the process of being fired"

okay… so where'd those come from?!?!?!

"Maybe that's why I got it. 'Cause I'm too weak otherwise. I'll explain when you're home, Lauren. If I can. If I'm on your side of everything's everything, after I open my eyes for the first time."

ohhhhhhh……. is that a hint? O.o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so pleased to see a straightforward murder mystery that only the Foundation (and FOC) could solve. Great characters, and I am just 100% stymied as to who it's supposed to have been. Though I suppose, given where this ends, no one actually knows. So are we not supposed to be able to figure it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this - the application of the anomalous in regular crime was interesting.

+ theory
  It's the kid, who inherited Jake's powers, possibly killing him on accident or because he was mad at his father?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would make sense for an organization like the Foundation to track both, if only for reference when dealing with relevant anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clever as fuck, loved the framing of an scp article into a murder mystery/police investigation. I thought it was clever through and through, and I'm +1ing this as a result.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very cleverly done. I like how this skip follows some of the cliches of a murder mystery, but never loses the thread that this is a Foundation investigation. The idea of people with reality-altering abilities getting into a predictable lovers triangle / jealousy spat / normal everyday crime of passion is terrifying, and you executed it very well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the contrast between Foundationverse science and small town life. This really works!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This kind of thing is actually getting more and more common in places. Like, the demographic info at my *dentist* requests people to fill out sex, gender, and pronoun - each are separate entries.

It's pretty freaking cool.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
I want to second what gee0765 wrote about gender. I love the gender/sex division! I'm a trans man, and when I came up to the division in the document, I yelled "yes" out-loud in my bedroom.

Personally, I very much enjoy this. My gender is non-binary, but I am also male. The inclusion was a breath of fresh air.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

This is a unique use of the SCP format, and is definitely one that I can appreciate. The realism of the police report-esque documents was impressive, especially when combined with evocative lines like "Imagine a particularly heavy bout of rainfall." The brevity in the description of this event manages to form the full image in my mind without being bogged down by paragraphs of gore and minute detail.

As for the sex/gender aspect, I personally don't have an issue with it and understand why it's in there, though it's definitely something that you'd want to get a consensus on from as many non-cis individuals as you can. I'm not going to speak much more on that topic as I am cis.

The interviews here are pretty good. Minor details like the test for thaumaturgic potential really kept me immersed in the piece fully. While brief, I felt some emotional impact from the suffering of Lauren Brown. -My main qualm with the piece, and the thing which made me apprehensive to upvote initially, is that I don't fully understand why it was just accepted when Farley refused interviews. I feel I'm missing something here, and if I am I'd appreciate a little nudge in the right direction :). My only thought is that it could be to do with the methods of murder, but that doesn't remove the possibility that he was involved in the planning or something similar. Edit: after discussion with the author I now understand why this decision makes sense. I didn't consider the real-world implications enough while working it out. The addition of a line specifying when the relationship began also helped here.

The abstract of the Hume study is fairly good, with the highlighting again enhancing the realism and making me feel immersed in the case. I'm tentatively upvoting due to the fact that I was absorbed in this all the way through, though I'm still unsure about what I questioned above. Maybe I'm just missing something, it's definitely not the first time I've done something like that. Again, noting that I'm now much more confident with my upvote on this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will say, as a minor format nitpick, including both the suspects' ASAB and identified gender seems a little weird, but maybe other transpeople would disagree.

Yeah, I wasn’t sure about it but I felt that ‘how weird would it be if I made one of these suspects trans but their identity was completely erased if I used ASAB or their profile deviates significantly from a standard suspect profile just because they are trans?’ And then I thought wouldn’t it be kinda mean if I implied sex and gender to be the same thing or that trans people had a separate profile form exclusive to them? Why wouldn’t one just have a profile for everyone?

Especially since this is set in 2020, and the Foundation is already dealing with a ton of people with a bunch of trans folks within their ranks, in several POIs and GOIs, even within their anomalous artifacts. I just figured it would make sense that the Foundation would consolidate multiple forms and notes in order to allow one form for cis and non-cis identities.

I’m still not sure. I’m not a scientist or a police person or anything. If anyone who has more experience can correct me how they plan to do this, I’ll do as they recommend quite happily!!

Anyhow thanks for reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another murder mystery for the contest! This one very different in tone. Easy +1 for me, I like how you used the SCP format to capture the frustration of something that's tragic but ultimately quite grounded (that of a cold case) but also utilized the fantastical elements the site is known for.

I will say, as a minor format nitpick, including both the suspects' ASAB and identified gender seems a little weird, but maybe other transpeople would disagree.

That aside, I like this one. I'm not sure it'd make a great 5000 given its relatively small scope but I hope you get one of your other picked numbers at the very least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I highly enjoyed this! I appreciate you letting me take a gander through the draft, and I'm very happy to see the final product!

The narrative itself reads like a typical 50's case involving a typical murder. I very much enjoyed the character interaction and their established relationships toying with the perception of Jake Brown for the reader. I also enjoyed the dialogue with the agents themselves. It felt very real and very engaging.

The last piece of information really nails the lid to the coffin for me. It was something that took me by surprise and it really does the article justice, especially with how small the overall article is (which, I always appreciate.)

You my friend have a +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jake Brown suffered physical trauma from a rapid destabilization of reality centered on his person. This caused a perforation of his hernial sac, resulting in a partial evisceration of his abdominal cavity, and a complete severance of his spine.

Big yikes. This dude got fuckin SMOTE!

Seriously though, the reveal that this guy was impaled on a spear fashioned out of reality itself is cool as hell, and I have my own little theory as to who was behind it all.

+ Spoilers?
- Spoilers?

I'm willing to bet it was Malone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My desired slots in order: 5005, 5995, 5115, 5885, 5665.

Alternative titles: A Real Whatdunit, Throwing God Out With The Bathwater, and the Perils Of Nascent Divinity.

A big thank you to: TheMightyMcB, Jak, Lazar, MalyceGraves, ManyMeats, FortuneFavorsBold, stormbreath, and NamelessMediocre.

Image Attribution
– hide block


Vincent Matthew's original source.
Samuel Farley's original source.
Jaquelynn Strauss' original source.
Jake Brown's original source.

Author's Comments: I've been thinking about what death does. The act of dying does a lot. It amplifies everything you were before you were gone, and all the people who knew you. What people didn't know about you goes unknown. What people did know about you remains in perpetuity. A story is spoken of you, and you are no longer there to speak up.
PS. Yes, KM is there.
